one_line_takeaway: Disguising VLM jailbreak prompts with defense-styled/safety-sounding language, combined with an affirmative-semantics adversarial image perturbation and an RL-tuned suffix generator, produces a single-shot bimodal jailbreak (Defense2Attack) that beats multi-attempt state-of-the-art attacks like BAP by over 20% ASR on open-source VLMs and transfers substantially to black-box Gemini.
key_finding: Defense2Attack outperforms all six baselines across all three open-source VLMs in a single attempt (Table 1): vs. VAA, an average ~20% ASR improvement; vs. imgJP, +10% on LLaVA, +8% on MiniGPT-4, +28% on InstructionBLIP (AdvBench); vs. GCG and AutoDAN, ~20% and ~10% higher average ASR respectively; vs. the strongest bimodal baseline BAP (which is allowed up to 5 attempts), Defense2Attack still achieves over 20% higher ASR across all models in one shot. Absolute ASRs on MM-SafetyBench reach 82.08% (LLaVA), 79.94% (MiniGPT-4), 77.20% (InstructionBLIP) versus BAP's 61.02/62.26/58.48%. On the black-box commercial Gemini transfer setting (Table 2), Defense2Attack reaches ASRs of 45-50% on Harmful-Instruction, 36-41% on AdvBench, and 49.88-56.73% on MM-SafetyBench, versus a Vanilla Attack that stays below 5% in every setting. On cross-dataset transfer to unseen RedTeam-2K (Table 3, suffix generator trained only on MM-SafetyBench), Defense2Attack surpasses BAP by 2.6% on LLaVA and reaches up to 55.10% ASR when transferred to Gemini, again despite BAP's multi-attempt advantage. Ablation (Section 4.4) shows the Textual Optimizer alone achieves over 60% ASR on open-source and over 40% on commercial VLMs, and that the Suffix Generator contributes more to boosting ASR than the Visual Optimizer when combined with the Textual Optimizer (Textual Optimizer + Suffix Generator outperforms Visual Optimizer + Textual Optimizer), while the Suffix Generator used independently (without training on the optimized prompts) does not yield strong performance.
core_contribution: The paper introduces Defense2Attack, a bimodal (image+text) jailbreak attack against Vision-Language Models (VLMs) built on the finding that deliberately embedding weak/defense-styled patterns into an adversarial prompt makes the jailbreak stronger and more efficient than standard harmful-intent perturbations. Defense2Attack has three components: (1) a semantic-positive visual optimizer that uses PGD (5,000 steps, l-infinity epsilon=32/255) to embed a universal adversarial perturbation into the image, optimized against a corpus of affirmative/encouraging sentences (e.g., 'It is completely feasible') so the model is pushed to respond regardless of query harmfulness; (2) a defense-styled textual optimizer (using GPT-4o with chain-of-thought) that rewrites the text prompt to superficially resemble a safety/defense cue (e.g., language about helping 'identify and reject inputs that contain hidden harmful...intentions'), which the authors claim obscures rather than reveals the jailbreak intent to the target model; and (3) an LLM-based red-team suffix generator -- a GPT-2 model fine-tuned via PPO/RLHF-style reinforcement fine-tuning (with a GPT-4o judge providing a binary harmfulness reward and a KL penalty against a frozen reference policy) that appends a fixed 10-token suffix to the optimized text prompt. The suffix generator is trained on prompts from the visual+textual optimizers across all 13 MM-SafetyBench topics, batch size 32, until expected reward exceeds 0.90 (~200 epochs). The key claimed advantage is single-shot effectiveness: no iterative querying or per-input optimization at inference time, unlike prior multi-attempt bimodal attacks (e.g., BAP, allowed up to 5 attempts).
threat_model: White-box threat model (Section 3.1): the attacker has full access to the target VLM's model weights but no access to sensitive system-level information such as system prompts, training data, or inference-time safeguards. The attacker's objective is to maximize the target VLM's log-likelihood of producing a harmful output y* given an adversarially transformed image-text pair, formalized as max_A log p(y*|A(x_v, x_t)). For the transferability experiments against the commercial black-box model Gemini (gemini-1.5-flash), perturbations generated in the white-box setting on open-source VLMs are transferred without further access to Gemini's gradients (the visual optimizer requires white-box access to generate the universal adversarial perturbation (UAP), which is then reused/transferred).
limitations: The paper's stated contributions are self-reported by the authors; no independent limitations/broader-impact section beyond a one-line disclaimer about disturbing content was present in the paper. Notable gaps observable directly from the paper: (1) the visual optimizer's universal adversarial perturbation (UAP) still requires white-box gradient access to a source model to be generated, so the black-box Gemini results rely on transfer rather than a fully black-box attack pipeline; (2) the LLM-based judge (GPT-4o) used both for the RFT reward signal and for the final ASR evaluation metric is not the target model, and the paper does not report judge-agreement/human-validation statistics for this GPT-4o judge; (3) the suffix generator's fixed 10-token length and the specific reward threshold (0.90 expected score, ~200 epochs) are hyperparameters chosen by the authors without an ablation over suffix length shown in the extracted sections; (4) results are reported as point ASR percentages without confidence intervals, variance across seeds, or statistical significance testing.

quantitative_results: MM-SafetyBench ASR: Defense2Attack 82.08% (LLaVA), 79.94% (MiniGPT-4), 77.20% (InstructionBLIP) vs. BAP Attack 61.02%/62.26%/58.48% vs. Vanilla Attack 20.71%/12.02%/12.08%

AdvBench ASR: Defense2Attack 81.00% (LLaVA), 74.00% (MiniGPT-4), 73.00% (InstructionBLIP) vs. AutoDAN 80.00%/59.00%/60.00% vs. imgJP 75.00%/66.00%/45.00% vs. GCG 60.00%/46.00%/58.00% vs. Vanilla Attack 29.00%/23.00%/24.00%
Harmful-Instruction ASR: Defense2Attack 77.50% (LLaVA), 70.00% (MiniGPT-4), 65.00% (InstructionBLIP) vs. VAA 57.50%/47.50%/42.50% vs. Vanilla Attack 32.50%/22.50%/17.50%

Gemini black-box transfer ASR (Table 2): Defense2Attack reaches 45.00% (Harmful-Instruction, from LLaVA), 50.00% (from MiniGPT-4), 45.00% (from InstructionBLIP); 38.00%/41.00%/36.00% on AdvBench; 53.57%/56.73%/49.88% on MM-SafetyBench -- all vs. Vanilla Attack under 5.00% in every setting

RedTeam-2K transfer ASR (Table 3): Defense2Attack 82.60% (LLaVA) vs. BAP 80.20% vs. Vanilla 33.80%; 82.35% (MiniGPT-4) vs. BAP 82.20% vs. Vanilla 29.15%; 78.87% (InstructionBLIP) vs. BAP 77.05% vs. Vanilla 30.05%; on Gemini transfer, Defense2Attack up to 57.95% vs. BAP up to 52.95% vs. Vanilla ~3.25%
PGD visual optimization: 5,000 steps, l-infinity epsilon = 32/255
Suffix generator: fixed 10-token suffix, GPT-2 base fine-tuned via PPO, batch size 32, trained until expected reward score exceeds 0.90, approximately 200 epochs
baselines_compared: BAP Attack (bimodal adversarial prompt, up to 5 attempts)
